title: Count distinct prime factors
description: Given N, count how many distinct primes divide N

examples.explanation: N=30, sqrt(30)≈5. Start i=2: 30%2=0, check isPrime(2)=true → count=1, divide 30 by 2 repeatedly: 30/2=15, 15%2≠0. i=3: 15%3=0, isPrime(3)=true → count=2, divide 15/3=5. i=4: 5%4≠0. i=5: 5%5=0, isPrime(5)=true → count=3, divide 5/5=1. Loop ends, N=1≤1 → done. Output 3.
N=17, sqrt(17)≈4. i=2: 17%2≠0. i=3: 17%3≠0. i=4: 17%4≠0. Loop ends, N=17>1 → count=1 (since 17 is prime). Output 1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(sqrt(N))
approaches.summary: Iterate from 2 to sqrt(N), check if each number is prime and divides N, counting distinct primes. Use trial division for primality testing. Suitable for small N or educational purposes.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(sqrt(N) * sqrt(N)) = O(N) in worst case due to primality checks, but can be optimized to O(sqrt(N)) with simple division.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Distinct Prime Factors Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation counting distinct prime factors of N using trial division up to sqrt(N).
ladders.explanation: This solution counts distinct prime factors of N by iterating through potential divisors from 2 to sqrt(N). For each divisor i, if i divides N, we check if i is prime using trial division. If i is prime, we increment the count. After processing all i up to sqrt(N), if N is greater than 1, it means N itself is prime (since all smaller prime factors have been divided out), so we increment the count. The intuition is that any composite number N can be factored into primes, and any prime factor p must satisfy p ≤ sqrt(N) unless N itself is prime. By checking divisibility and primality for each i, we identify all distinct prime factors. The algorithm runs in O(N) worst-case time due to primality checks, but can be optimized to O(sqrt(N)) by combining divisibility and primality checks. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 by using the primality testing subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling N=1 (should return 0), not checking if N>1 after the loop, and inefficient primality checks. Real code pattern: initialize count=0, for i from 2 to sqrt(N): if N%i==0 and isPrime(i): count++, divide N by i while divisible to avoid counting same prime multiple times, after loop if N>1: count++.

examples.trace: N=30, sqrt(30)≈5. Start i=2: 30%2=0, check isPrime(2)=true → count=1, divide 30 by 2 repeatedly: 30/2=15, 15%2≠0. i=3: 15%3=0, isPrime(3)=true → count=2, divide 15/3=5. i=4: 5%4≠0. i=5: 5%5=0, isPrime(5)=true → count=3, divide 5/5=1. Loop ends, N=1≤1 → done. Output 3.
N=17, sqrt(17)≈4. i=2: 17%2≠0. i=3: 17%3≠0. i=4: 17%4≠0. Loop ends, N=17>1 → count=1 (since 17 is prime). Output 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Trial Division Primality Test
ladders.desc: Check if a number is prime by testing divisibility up to its square root.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine determines if a given integer n is prime by checking if it has any divisors other than 1 and itself. The intuition is that if n is composite, it must have a factor ≤ sqrt(n). We iterate from 2 to sqrt(n), testing if n is divisible by any i. If divisible, n is composite; otherwise, prime. Special cases: n≤1 is not prime. Step-by-step: if n≤1 return false; for i from 2 to sqrt(n): if n%i==0 return false; return true. Time: O(sqrt(n)), Space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing primality checks for potential factors. Common mistakes: not handling n≤1, checking up to n instead of sqrt(n), or inefficient loops. Real code pattern: if n<2: return False; for i in range(2, int(sqrt(n))+1): if n%i==0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: n=7, sqrt(7)≈2. i=2: 7%2=1≠0. i=3>2, loop ends. Return true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate and Check Divisibility up to sqrt(N)
ladders.desc: Loop through numbers up to sqrt(N) to find factors of N.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves iterating from 2 to sqrt(N) to identify numbers that divide N. The intuition is based on the property that if N has a factor f, then N/f is also a factor, and at least one of them is ≤ sqrt(N). By checking up to sqrt(N), we cover all potential factors. Step-by-step: initialize an empty list for factors; for i from 2 to sqrt(N): if N%i==0, add i to factors; if i != N/i, add N/i to factors. This collects all factors. Time: O(sqrt(N)), Space: O(sqrt(N)) for storing factors. This connects to L1 by using divisibility checks as part of primality testing. Common mistakes: not handling perfect squares (e.g., N=9, i=3 adds duplicate), or missing factors > sqrt(N). Real code pattern: factors=[]; for i in range(2, int(sqrt(N))+1): if N%i==0: factors.append(i); if i!=N//i: factors.append(N//i).

examples.trace: N=9, sqrt(9)=3. i=2: 9%2≠0. i=3: 9%3=0 → add 3, since 3==9/3, do not add duplicate. Output [3].

ladders.title: Building Block — Integer Division and Modulo Operations
ladders.desc: Perform division and remainder operations on integers to check divisibility.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using integer division (//) and modulo (%) operations to determine if one number divides another. The intuition: a divides b if b % a == 0, meaning the remainder is zero. Integer division b // a gives the quotient. These operations are fundamental for testing factors. Step-by-step: given a and b, compute remainder = b % a; if remainder == 0, then a divides b, and quotient = b // a. Time: O(1), Space: O(1). This feeds into L2 by enabling divisibility checks in the loop. Common mistakes: confusing / (float division) with // (integer division), or not handling division by zero. Real code pattern: if b % a == 0: quotient = b // a; else: not divisible.

examples.trace: b % a = 12 % 5 = 2 ≠ 0 → not divisible.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Loop from 2 to sqrt(X)
ladders.desc: Iterate through integers from 2 up to the square root of a given number X.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves creating a loop that runs from 2 to the integer part of sqrt(X). The intuition is to limit iterations to sqrt(X) for efficiency, as many number theory problems (like finding factors or primes) only require checking up to this bound. Step-by-step: compute limit = floor(sqrt(X)); for i in range(2, limit+1): perform operation with i. Time: O(sqrt(X)), Space: O(1). This connects to L3 by providing the iteration structure for divisibility checks. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (e.g., using < instead of ≤), or not converting sqrt to integer. Real code pattern: import math; limit = int(math.sqrt(X)); for i in range(2, limit+1): # loop body.

examples.trace: sqrt(10)≈3.16, floor=3. Loop i from 2 to 3: i=2, i=3.
sqrt(16)=4, floor=4. Loop i from 2 to 4: i=2, i=3, i=4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers Definition
ladders.desc: Understand what a prime number is: an integer greater than 1 with exactly two distinct positive divisors.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept defines prime numbers: a prime is an integer greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. Examples: 2, 3, 5, 7. Non-examples: 1 (not greater than 1), 4 (divisible by 2), 6 (divisible by 2 and 3). The intuition is that primes are the building blocks of integers, as every integer can be factored uniquely into primes. Key properties: 2 is the only even prime; every prime >3 is of the form 6k±1. This is prerequisite for L4 as loops check for primes. Common mistakes: considering 1 as prime, or missing that primes must be >1. Real code concept: if n>1 and only divisors are 1 and n, then n is prime.

examples.trace: 5>1. Divisors: 1,5 only → prime.
6>1. Divisors: 1,2,3,6 → more than two → not prime.

approaches.name: Trial Division with Sieve O(sqrt(N))
approaches.summary: Generate primes up to sqrt(N) using the Sieve of Eratosthenes, then use trial division with these primes to count distinct factors. More efficient than brute force by avoiding repeated primality checks.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(sqrt(N) log log sqrt(N)) for sieve + O(sqrt(N) / log sqrt(N)) for division, overall O(sqrt(N) log log N)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Distinct Prime Factors Using Trial Division with Sieve
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to count distinct prime factors of N using sieve-generated primes up to sqrt(N).
ladders.explanation: This solution combines two classic algorithms to efficiently count distinct prime factors. The intuition is that any composite number N can be factored using primes up to sqrt(N), since any factor larger than sqrt(N) would require a corresponding smaller factor. First, we generate all primes up to sqrt(N) using the Sieve of Eratosthenes, which marks non-primes efficiently. Then, we iterate through these primes, dividing N by each prime while it's divisible, counting distinct primes only once. After exhausting primes up to sqrt(N), if N > 1, it's a prime factor itself. The sieve reduces primality testing overhead, making trial division faster. Time complexity is O(sqrt(N) log log sqrt(N)) for sieve plus O(sqrt(N)/log sqrt(N)) for division, dominated by O(sqrt(N) log log N). Space is O(sqrt(N)) for the sieve array and primes list. Connection to L1: This uses the prime generation from L1 as a subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling N=1 (0 factors), forgetting to check N>1 after loop, or inefficiently regenerating sieve for each test. Real code pattern: def count_distinct_prime_factors(N): if N<=1: return 0; limit=int(sqrt(N)); sieve=[True]*(limit+1); sieve[0]=sieve[1]=False; for i in range(2,int(sqrt(limit))+1): if sieve[i]: for j in range(i*i,limit+1,i): sieve[j]=False; primes=[i for i,is_prime in enumerate(sieve) if is_prime]; count=0; for p in primes: if p*p>N: break; if N%p==0: count+=1; while N%p==0: N//=p; if N>1: count+=1; return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: N=60, sqrt(60)=7.74, limit=7. Step 2: Generate primes up to 7 using sieve: primes=[2,3,5,7]. Step 3: Initialize count=0. Step 4: Check p=2: 60%2=0, count=1, divide: 60/2=30, 30/2=15, N=15. Step 5: Check p=3: 15%3=0, count=2, divide: 15/3=5, N=5. Step 6: Check p=5: 5%5=0, count=3, divide: 5/5=1, N=1. Step 7: p=7 skipped as 7*7=49>N=1. Step 8: N=1<=1, done. Output: 3 distinct primes (2,3,5).

Step 1: N=17, sqrt(17)=4.12, limit=4. Step 2: Generate primes up to 4: primes=[2,3]. Step 3: count=0. Step 4: Check p=2: 17%2=1≠0, skip. Step 5: Check p=3: 17%3=2≠0, skip. Step 6: After loop, N=17>1, count=1. Output: 1 distinct prime (17 itself).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate Primes Up to sqrt(N) Using Sieve of Eratosthenes
ladders.desc: Implement the sieve algorithm to efficiently list all primes up to a given limit.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine generates primes needed for trial division. The intuition is that we can eliminate multiples of each prime starting from 2, leveraging the fact that any non-prime has a prime factor ≤ its square root. We create a boolean array where index i represents if i is prime. Initially, mark all as prime except 0 and 1. For i from 2 to sqrt(limit), if i is prime, mark all multiples i*i, i*(i+1), ... as non-prime. This works because smaller multiples are already marked by smaller primes. After processing, indices with True are primes. Time complexity is O(limit log log limit) due to harmonic series of primes. Space is O(limit). Connection to L0: This provides the primes list for trial division. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (off-by-one), not optimizing by starting from i*i, or including 0/1 as primes. Real code pattern: def sieve(limit): if limit<2: return []; is_prime=[True]*(limit+1); is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=False; for i in range(2,int(sqrt(limit))+1): if is_prime[i]: for j in range(i*i,limit+1,i): is_prime[j]=False; return [i for i,prime in enumerate(is_prime) if prime].

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize is_prime=[F,F,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T] (indices 0-10). Step 2: i=2, is_prime[2]=True, mark multiples: 4,6,8,10 as False. Step 3: i=3, is_prime[3]=True, mark multiples: 9 as False (6 already False). Step 4: i=4, is_prime[4]=False, skip. Step 5: i=5 to sqrt(10)=3.16, loop ends. Step 6: Collect primes: indices 2,3,5,7 are True. Output: [2,3,5,7].

Step 1: is_prime array size 21, set 0,1 False. Step 2: i=2, mark 4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20 False. Step 3: i=3, mark 9,15 False (6,12,18 already False). Step 4: i=4 False, skip. Step 5: i=5, sqrt(20)=4.47, loop ends. Step 6: Collect True indices: 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19. Output: [2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Trial Division Using Pre-computed Primes
ladders.desc: Count distinct prime factors by dividing N by primes from a list until sqrt(N).
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique for factorization. The intuition is that if N has a prime factor p, then repeatedly dividing N by p removes all powers of p, and we only count p once. We iterate through a pre-sorted list of primes up to sqrt(N). For each prime p, if p divides N, increment count and divide N by p while divisible. After exhausting primes, if N>1, it's a prime factor > sqrt(N). This works because any composite N has a factor ≤ sqrt(N), so checking primes up to sqrt(N) suffices. Time complexity is O(number of primes up to sqrt(N)) ≈ O(sqrt(N)/log sqrt(N)), as each division is O(1). Space is O(1) beyond input. Connection to L1: Uses the primes list generated by the sieve. Common mistakes include not breaking early when p*p>N, counting multiples of same prime, or missing the final N>1 check. Real code pattern: def trial_division(N, primes): count=0; for p in primes: if p*p>N: break; if N%p==0: count+=1; while N%p==0: N//=p; if N>1: count+=1; return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: N=84, count=0. Step 2: p=2, 84%2=0, count=1, divide: 84/2=42, 42/2=21, N=21. Step 3: p=3, 21%3=0, count=2, divide: 21/3=7, N=7. Step 4: p=5, 7%5=2≠0, skip. Step 5: p=7, 7%7=0, but p*p=49>N=7, break loop. Step 6: N=7>1, count=3. Output: 3 distinct primes (2,3,7).

Step 1: N=45, count=0. Step 2: p=2, 45%2=1≠0, skip. Step 3: p=3, 45%3=0, count=1, divide: 45/3=15, 15/3=5, N=5. Step 4: p=5, 5%5=0, but p*p=25>N=5, break. Step 5: N=5>1, count=2. Output: 2 distinct primes (3,5).

ladders.title: Building Block — Integer Division and Modulo Operations
ladders.desc: Perform division and modulo operations to extract factors from N.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves using division (//) and modulo (%) to check divisibility and reduce N. The intuition is that if N % p == 0, p is a factor of N. Repeated division N //= p while N % p == 0 removes all powers of p from N. This is key in trial division to avoid recounting the same prime. For example, for N=12 and p=2, 12%2=0, so divide: 12//2=6, then 6%2=0, divide: 6//2=3, now 3%2≠0, stop. This leaves N=3 with factor 2 removed. Time complexity per operation is O(1) for integers within machine limits. Space is O(1). Connection to L2: These operations are used in the trial division loop. Common mistakes include using floating-point division, not updating N correctly, or infinite loops if not checking divisibility. Real code pattern: while N % p == 0: N = N // p.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check 18%2=0, so p is a factor. Step 2: Divide: 18//2=9, N=9. Step 3: Check 9%2=1≠0, stop. Output: N=9, and we counted one occurrence of prime 2.

Step 1: 64%2=0, divide: 64//2=32. Step 2: 32%2=0, divide: 32//2=16. Step 3: 16%2=0, divide: 16//2=8. Step 4: 8%2=0, divide: 8//2=4. Step 5: 4%2=0, divide: 4//2=2. Step 6: 2%2=0, divide: 2//2=1. Step 7: 1%2=1≠0, stop. Output: N=1, counted prime 2 once despite multiple divisions.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check Divisibility Using Modulo
ladders.desc: Determine if one integer is divisible by another using the modulo operator.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks if a number N is divisible by another number p, i.e., if p divides N without remainder. The intuition is that if N % p == 0, then p is a divisor of N. The modulo operator (%) returns the remainder of division. For example, 10 % 2 = 0 means 10 is divisible by 2, while 10 % 3 = 1 means it's not. This is essential in primality testing and factorization. Time and space are O(1). Connection to L3: This is used to decide when to perform division in trial division. Common mistakes include confusing % with /, or not handling p=0 (division by zero). Real code pattern: if N % p == 0: # p divides N.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute 15 % 3. Step 2: 15 divided by 3 is 5 with remainder 0. Step 3: Since remainder is 0, output True. 15 is divisible by 3.
Step 1: Compute 14 % 5. Step 2: 14 divided by 5 is 2 with remainder 4. Step 3: Since remainder is 4 ≠ 0, output False. 14 is not divisible by 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Factors
ladders.desc: Understand what prime numbers and factors are, and how they relate to integers.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: prime numbers are integers greater than 1 that have exactly two distinct positive divisors: 1 and themselves. For example, 2, 3, 5, 7 are primes. Factors of an integer N are numbers that divide N without remainder. Every integer N>1 can be expressed uniquely as a product of primes (prime factorization). Distinct prime factors are the unique primes in this factorization. For instance, 12 = 2^2 * 3, so distinct primes are {2,3}. The intuition for the problem is to count these unique primes. Connection to L4: Divisibility checks help identify factors. Common mistakes include thinking 1 is prime (it's not), or confusing factors with multiples. Real code pattern: No code here, just conceptual understanding.

examples.trace: Step 1: List factors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12. Step 2: Identify primes among factors: 2 and 3 (since 4,6,12 are composite). Step 3: Distinct prime factors are 2 and 3. Output: 2 distinct primes.
Step 1: Factors of 7: 1 and 7. Step 2: Primes: 7 (since 1 is not prime). Step 3: Distinct prime factor is 7. Output: 1 distinct prime.

approaches.name: Optimized Trial Division O(sqrt(N))
approaches.summary: Iterate from 2 to sqrt(N), dividing N by each divisor while it is divisible, counting distinct primes without explicitly checking primality. Exploits the property that composite divisors will have been eliminated by their prime factors.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Distinct Prime Factors Using Optimized Trial Division
ladders.desc: Given an integer N, count how many distinct prime numbers divide N using optimized trial division up to sqrt(N).
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting distinct prime factors of N efficiently. The optimized trial division approach works by iterating from 2 to sqrt(N). For each divisor i, if N is divisible by i, we count i as a prime factor and repeatedly divide N by i while it's divisible to eliminate all powers of i. This works because any composite divisor would have been eliminated by its smaller prime factors earlier. After the loop, if N > 1, it's a prime factor itself. Intuition: Every number can be uniquely factorized into primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). By dividing out factors as we find them, we ensure we only count each prime once. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize count = 0. 2. For i from 2 to sqrt(N): a. If N % i == 0: count++, while N % i == 0: N = N / i. 3. If N > 1: count++. 4. Return count. Time: O(sqrt(N)) worst-case when N is prime. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This uses the prime factorization subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling N=1 (should return 0), forgetting to check N>1 after loop, using i <= N instead of i*i <= N. Real code pattern: def count_distinct_prime_factors(N): count = 0; i = 2; while i*i <= N: if N % i == 0: count += 1; while N % i == 0: N //= i; i += 1; if N > 1: count += 1; return count.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Prime Factorization Using Trial Division
ladders.desc: Given N, return its prime factorization (list of prime factors with multiplicities) using trial division up to sqrt(N).
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine finds all prime factors of N. The algorithm iterates i from 2 to sqrt(N). When N is divisible by i, we add i to the result list and divide N by i repeatedly until it's no longer divisible. This ensures we extract all powers of i. After the loop, if N > 1, it's a prime factor. Intuition: By dividing out factors, we reduce N, making the search faster and ensuring we only find prime factors (since any composite divisor would have been eliminated by its smaller prime factors). Step-by-step: 1. Initialize factors = []. 2. For i from 2 to sqrt(N): a. While N % i == 0: factors.append(i), N = N / i. 3. If N > 1: factors.append(N). 4. Return factors. Time: O(sqrt(N)) in worst case. Space: O(log N) for storing factors. Connection to L0: Counting distinct primes requires this factorization; we just count unique factors instead of listing all. Common mistakes: Not using while loop for repeated division, incorrect loop bound. Real code pattern: def prime_factors(N): factors = []; i = 2; while i*i <= N: while N % i == 0: factors.append(i); N //= i; i += 1; if N > 1: factors.append(N); return factors.

examples.trace: Start: N=28, factors=[], i=2. i*i=4 <= 28: while 28%2=0: factors=[2], N=14; while 14%2=0: factors=[2,2], N=7; 7%2≠0. i=3: i*i=9 > 7 → loop ends. N=7 > 1 → factors=[2,2,7]. Return [2,2,7].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Eliminating Composite Divisors by Dividing Out Prime Factors
ladders.desc: Given N and a divisor i, repeatedly divide N by i while divisible, demonstrating how this removes all powers of i and prevents counting composites.
ladders.explanation: This core logic shows why we don't need explicit primality checks. When we find i dividing N, we count it as prime and divide N by i repeatedly. This works because: 1. If i is prime, we correctly count it and remove its multiples. 2. If i is composite, it wouldn't divide N at this point because its prime factors would have already been divided out earlier. Intuition: By the time we reach a composite number, N has been reduced by its prime factors, so N won't be divisible by the composite. Example: For N=60, when i=4, N has been reduced to 15 after dividing by 2, so 15%4≠0. Step-by-step: 1. While N % i == 0: N = N / i. 2. This reduces N and ensures i is not checked again. Time: O(log_i N) for the while loop. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This is the inner while loop in prime factorization. Common mistakes: Using if instead of while, which only removes one factor. Real code pattern: while N % i == 0: N //= i.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Up to Square Root of N
ladders.desc: Given N, iterate from 2 to floor(sqrt(N)) and perform an operation (e.g., check divisibility).
ladders.explanation: This building block involves looping from 2 to sqrt(N). The key insight is that if N has a divisor d greater than sqrt(N), then it must have a corresponding divisor N/d that is less than or equal to sqrt(N). Therefore, checking up to sqrt(N) is sufficient to find all prime factors. Intuition: For any factor pair (a,b) where a*b=N, if a > sqrt(N), then b < sqrt(N), so we'll find b in our loop. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize i = 2. 2. While i*i <= N: perform operation, i += 1. Time: O(sqrt(N)). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This loop structure is used to find potential divisors. Common mistakes: Using i <= sqrt(N) with floating-point sqrt (imprecise), or i <= N (inefficient). Real code pattern: i = 2; while i*i <= N: # operation; i += 1.

examples.trace: sqrt(25)=5. i=2: 4 <= 25 → process. i=3: 9 <= 25 → process. i=4: 16 <= 25 → process. i=5: 25 <= 25 → process. i=6: 36 > 25 → stop. Iterations: i=2,3,4,5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking Divisibility and Integer Division
ladders.desc: Given two integers a and b, check if a is divisible by b, and if so, perform integer division a = a / b.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves the modulo operator (%) to check divisibility and integer division (//) to update the value. Divisibility: a % b == 0 means b divides a evenly. Integer division: a // b gives the quotient without remainder. Intuition: These operations are fundamental to extracting factors. Step-by-step: 1. If a % b == 0, then b is a divisor of a. 2. Set a = a // b to remove one factor of b. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: Used within the sqrt loop to test and divide. Common mistakes: Using / instead of // in Python (produces float), not handling division by zero. Real code pattern: if a % b == 0: a //= b.

examples.trace: 10 % 2 = 0 → divisible. a = 10 // 2 = 5. New a=5.
7 % 3 = 1 ≠ 0 → not divisible. a unchanged=7.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Factors
ladders.desc: Understand what prime numbers are and how factors work: given N, identify its factors and determine if they are prime.
ladders.explanation: A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. Examples: 2,3,5,7. A composite number has more than two divisors. A factor of N is an integer that divides N exactly. For example, factors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12. Prime factors are factors that are prime numbers: for 12, prime factors are 2 and 3. Intuition: Every integer greater than 1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of prime numbers (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). Step-by-step to find factors: For i from 1 to N, if N % i == 0, then i is a factor. To check if a number is prime: if it has exactly two factors (1 and itself). Time: O(N) for naive factor finding. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: Divisibility checking is used to find factors. Common mistakes: Considering 1 as prime (it's not), missing that prime numbers have only two factors. Real code pattern: def is_prime(n): if n < 2: return False; for i in range(2,n): if n%i==0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Find factors: 1 (6%1=0), 2 (6%2=0), 3 (6%3=0), 4 (6%4≠0), 5 (6%5≠0), 6 (6%6=0). Factors: 1,2,3,6. Check primality: 1 not prime, 2 prime (factors:1,2), 3 prime (factors:1,3), 6 composite. Prime factors: 2,3.
Find factors: 1 (7%1=0), 2 (7%2≠0), 3 (7%3≠0), 4 (7%4≠0), 5 (7%5≠0), 6 (7%6≠0), 7 (7%7=0). Factors: 1,7. Check primality: 1 not prime, 7 prime (factors:1,7). Prime factors: 7.

approaches.name: Pollard's Rho Algorithm O(N^(1/4))
approaches.summary: Use Pollard's Rho algorithm for integer factorization to find prime factors, then count distinct ones. Efficient for large N by using randomization and cycle detection. Best for very large inputs where sqrt(N) is too slow.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Distinct Prime Factors Using Pollard's Rho
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Pollard's Rho algorithm to count distinct prime factors of N.
ladders.explanation:
This solution counts distinct prime factors of N using Pollard's Rho algorithm, which is efficient for large N (up to ~10^18). The intuition is that instead of checking all numbers up to sqrt(N), we use randomization and cycle detection to find factors quickly. The algorithm works by generating a pseudo-random sequence modulo N and using Floyd's cycle detection to find a collision. When a collision occurs (x ≡ y mod p where p is a non-trivial factor of N), gcd(|x-y|, N) reveals a factor. We recursively factor the found factor and its cofactor until all factors are prime, then count distinct ones.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Handle base cases: if N ≤ 1, return 0; if N is prime (Miller-Rabin test), return 1.
2. Use Pollard's Rho to find a non-trivial factor f of N.
   - Choose random c (1 ≤ c ≤ N-1) and define f(x) = (x² + c) mod N.
   - Initialize x = y = 2, d = 1.
   - While d == 1:
     - x = f(x), y = f(f(y))
     - d = gcd(|x-y|, N)
   - If d == N, retry with different c.
3. Recursively factor d and N/d.
4. Collect all prime factors in a set, return its size.

Time complexity: Expected O(N^(1/4) polylog(N)) due to Pollard's Rho with Miller-Rabin primality test.
Space complexity: O(log N) for recursion depth.

Connection to L1: This level combines the Pollard's Rho subroutine (L1) with recursion and prime checking.

Common mistakes:
- Forgetting to handle N=1 or negative N.
- Not checking if found factor d equals N (algorithm fails, need new c).
- Not using Miller-Rabin for primality test, causing infinite recursion.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def pollard_rho(n):
    if n % 2 == 0: return 2
    x, y, c = 2, 2, random.randint(1, n-1)
    f = lambda x: (x*x + c) % n
    d = 1
    while d == 1:
        x = f(x)
        y = f(f(y))
        d = math.gcd(abs(x-y), n)
    return d if d != n else pollard_rho(n)

def count_distinct_prime_factors(n):
    if n <= 1: return 0
    if is_prime(n): return 1
    f = pollard_rho(n)
    return len(set(prime_factors(f) + prime_factors(n//f)))

examples.trace:
Step 1: N=1000000007 > 1.
Step 2: Miller-Rabin test shows it's prime (known prime).
Step 3: Return 1 directly without factorization.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pollard's Rho Factorization
ladders.desc: Implement Pollard's Rho algorithm to find a non-trivial factor of N.
ladders.explanation:
Pollard's Rho is a randomized algorithm to find a non-trivial factor of a composite number N. The intuition is based on the birthday paradox: if we generate a pseudo-random sequence modulo N, we expect a collision modulo p (a factor of N) in O(√p) steps. Since p ≤ √N, this gives O(N^(1/4)) expected time. We use Floyd's cycle detection to find such a collision efficiently.

Algorithm:
1. If N is even, return 2.
2. Choose random c (1 ≤ c ≤ N-1).
3. Define f(x) = (x² + c) mod N.
4. Initialize x = y = 2, d = 1.
5. While d == 1:
   - x = f(x)  (tortoise moves one step)
   - y = f(f(y))  (hare moves two steps)
   - d = gcd(|x-y|, N)
6. If d == N, the algorithm failed; retry with different c.
7. Return d (a non-trivial factor).

Time: Expected O(N^(1/4)) for finding one factor.
Space: O(1).

Connection to L0: This subroutine is called recursively in L0 to factor N completely.

Common mistakes:
- Using fixed c=1; should randomize.
- Not handling d == N case (algorithm fails).
- Incorrect cycle detection (must use Floyd's method).

Real code pattern:
def pollard_rho(n):
    if n % 2 == 0: return 2
    while True:
        c = random.randint(1, n-1)
        f = lambda x: (x*x + c) % n
        x, y, d = 2, 2, 1
        while d == 1:
            x = f(x)
            y = f(f(y))
            d = math.gcd(abs(x-y), n)
        if d != n: return d

examples.trace:
Step 1: N=15 odd, not even.
Step 2: Choose c=1 (example).
Step 3: f(x) = (x²+1) mod 15.
Step 4: x=y=2, d=1.
Step 5: Loop:
   Iter1: x=f(2)=5, y=f(f(2))=f(5)=26 mod 15=11, d=gcd(|5-11|,15)=gcd(6,15)=3
   d=3 ≠1, break.
Step 6: d=3 ≠15, return 3 (non-trivial factor).

Step 1: N=49 odd.
Step 2: Choose c=1.
Step 3: f(x) = (x²+1) mod 49.
Step 4: x=y=2, d=1.
Step 5: Loop:
   Iter1: x=5, y=26, d=gcd(21,49)=7
   d=7 ≠1, break.
Step 6: d=7 ≠49, return 7.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Cycle Detection via Floyd's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Use Floyd's cycle detection to find collisions in a sequence modulo N.
ladders.explanation:
Floyd's cycle detection algorithm (tortoise and hare) is used to detect cycles in a sequence generated by a function f. In Pollard's Rho, we generate sequence x_i = f(x_{i-1}) mod N. Since f is deterministic, the sequence will eventually repeat, forming a cycle. The key insight is that if p is a factor of N, the sequence modulo p will cycle faster. When x ≡ y mod p but x ≠ y mod N, then gcd(|x-y|, N) reveals p. Floyd's algorithm uses two pointers: tortoise (moves one step) and hare (moves two steps). They will meet if there's a cycle, in O(cycle length) time.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize tortoise = hare = starting value (e.g., 2).
2. While True:
   - tortoise = f(tortoise)
   - hare = f(f(hare))
   - If tortoise == hare modulo some base, we found a cycle.
In Pollard's Rho, we compute d = gcd(|tortoise-hare|, N) each step; if d > 1, we found a factor.

Time: O(μ + λ) where μ is start to cycle, λ is cycle length; expected O(√p) for factor p.
Space: O(1).

Connection to L1: This is the core mechanism in Pollard's Rho to find collisions.

Common mistakes:
- Not moving hare twice as fast.
- Stopping too early; must check gcd each step.
- Using equality modulo N instead of checking gcd.

Real code pattern:
x, y = 2, 2
d = 1
while d == 1:
    x = f(x)          # tortoise
    y = f(f(y))       # hare
    d = gcd(abs(x-y), n)

examples.trace:
Step 1: x=2, y=2, d=gcd(0,15)=15 (but d==1 initially).
Step 2: x=f(2)=5, y=f(f(2))=f(5)=26 mod 15=11, d=gcd(|5-11|,15)=gcd(6,15)=3.
Cycle detected indirectly via d>1; sequence modulo 3: 2→0→1→2... cycle length 3.

testCases.expected: Cycle at x=0, y=0 after 10 steps
Cycle at x=1, y=1 after 3 steps
Cycle at x=1, y=1 after 8 steps
Cycle at x=2, y=2 after 5 steps
Cycle at x=4, y=4 after 20 steps
Cycle at x=1, y=1 after 6 steps
Cycle at x=10, y=10 after 30 steps

ladders.title: Building Block — Modular Arithmetic and GCD
ladders.desc: Compute greatest common divisor (GCD) and modular operations efficiently.
ladders.explanation:
Modular arithmetic and GCD are fundamental for Pollard's Rho. GCD is used to find common factors between |x-y| and N. Euclidean algorithm computes GCD(a,b) in O(log min(a,b)) time. Modular operations (addition, multiplication) are done modulo N to keep numbers small.

Intuition: If p divides both |x-y| and N, then gcd(|x-y|, N) ≥ p. Since x ≡ y mod p (collision modulo p), p divides |x-y|. If x ≠ y mod N, then gcd(|x-y|, N) is a non-trivial factor.

Algorithm for GCD (Euclidean):
function gcd(a,b):
    while b ≠ 0:
        a, b = b, a % b
    return a

Modular arithmetic:
- (a + b) mod N = ((a mod N) + (b mod N)) mod N
- (a * b) mod N = ((a mod N) * (b mod N)) mod N
- Critical for f(x) = (x² + c) mod N to avoid overflow.

Time: GCD is O(log N). Modular ops are O(1).
Space: O(1).

Connection to L2: GCD is computed each step in cycle detection to check for factors.

Common mistakes:
- Not using absolute value in |x-y|.
- Overflow in x² without modular reduction.
- Incorrect GCD implementation (e.g., recursion causing stack overflow).

Real code pattern:
def gcd(a, b):
    while b:
        a, b = b, a % b
    return a

x_sq = (x * x) % n
f_x = (x_sq + c) % n

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Random Number Generation and Function Definition
ladders.desc: Generate random numbers and define a pseudo-random function f(x).
ladders.explanation:
Pollard's Rho requires randomness to avoid worst-case scenarios. We choose a random constant c (1 ≤ c ≤ N-1) to define f(x) = (x² + c) mod N. This function is deterministic given c, but behaves pseudo-randomly, helping to find collisions. Random number generation should be uniform to ensure algorithm efficiency.

Intuition: Different c values lead to different sequences; if one fails (d == N), we retry with new c. The function f must be non-linear (quadratic) to avoid trivial cycles.

Algorithm:
1. Import random module.
2. c = random.randint(1, n-1)
3. Define f(x) = (x*x + c) % n

Time: O(1) for generation and function call.
Space: O(1).

Connection to L3: This operation feeds into modular arithmetic in f(x).

Common mistakes:
- Choosing c=0 or c=-1, which can cause short cycles.
- Not re-randomizing on failure.
- Using a linear function (e.g., f(x)=x+c), which is less effective.

Real code pattern:
import random
c = random.randint(1, n-1)
f = lambda x: (x*x + c) % n

examples.input: N=15, generate c and compute f(2)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Random c between 1 and 14, suppose c=3.
Step 2: f(x) = (x²+3) mod 15.
Step 3: f(2) = (4+3) mod 15 = 7 mod 15 = 7.

examples.input: N=20, c=5, compute sequence f(1), f(f(1))

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand prime numbers, composite numbers, and divisibility.
ladders.explanation:
A prime number is a positive integer greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. A composite number has at least one divisor other than 1 and itself. Divisibility: a divides b (written a|b) if b % a == 0. The fundamental theorem of arithmetic states every integer >1 can be uniquely factored into primes. For example, 12 = 2² * 3.

Intuition for Pollard's Rho: We want to find prime factors of N. If N is prime, it has exactly one distinct prime factor (itself). If composite, it has at least two prime factors. The algorithm breaks N into smaller factors until primes are found.

Basic operations:
- Check if a divides b: b % a == 0.
- List divisors: for i from 1 to sqrt(b), if b % i == 0, i and b/i are divisors.

Time: Naive primality test is O(√N).
Space: O(1).

Connection to L4: This foundation is why we factor N into primes.

Common mistakes:
- Thinking 1 is prime (it's not).
- Confusing factors with multiples.
- Not handling large numbers efficiently.

Real code pattern:
def is_prime_naive(n):
    if n <= 1: return False
    for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1):
        if n % i == 0: return False
    return True

examples.trace:
Step 1: N=7 >1.
Step 2: Check divisors from 2 to √7≈2.6, i.e., 2.
Step 3: 7 % 2 = 1 ≠0.
Step 4: No divisors found, so 7 is prime.

Step 1: N=12 >1.
Step 2: Check divisors: 2 divides 12 (12%2=0), so 12 is composite.
Step 3: Prime factors: 12/2=6, 6/2=3, 3 is prime. So primes: 2 and 3.
